pages.1:
# 3- Interpretability

Machine Learning changed the way computer programs are done. Developers used to formalize a problem and code the rules that would transform input data into output data. Now, it is the model that determines the rules that link the input and output data through iterations and error minimization. It is this ability that gives all its power to Machine Learning and allows us to solve problems that exceed human formalization capabilities. But it is also coupled with a constraint: the rules applied by the model after training to make its predictions and decision may evade human control. For certain types of models, these rules sometimes remain unknown, even if interpretability techniques have made great progress in recent years. This is the famous "black box effect". However, when we decide to use Al to make decisions or make predictions that will have an impact on the lives of citizens, it is crucial, and soon to be regulatory, to keep control of the models and guarantee their explicability.

The interpretability of AI models is therefore becoming a major challenge, both for those who design them and for those who use them. It is a question of knowing which factors influence each decision and prediction (local interpretability) but also which rules govern the model in general (global interpretability).

Interpretability can be defined as having access to the features or variables that are driving the decisions or predictions of a model.

An Interpretable system becomes:

· Explicable: we can provide information and explanation (see the Explicability section)

· Transparent: it has a functioning intelligible to a human

· Provable: it can provide proof of the accuracy of the result

Whatever your use-case, it is critical to use interpretability methods.

If your system is critical, you should use directly interpretable models (regressions or simple decision trees), you can refer to Section 3.C.Direct Interpretability for more information.

If you are not required Direct Interpretability, are decided to use more complex models, you can use toolboxes to access indirect interpretability. You can refer to Section 3.D for more information, depending on the type of data (tabular, text or images).

## A. For whom?

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.2:
### A.1. The regulator

As mentioned in the section about legal risks in Introduction, the new European law on AI will describe different levels of criticality for AI models. Depending on the level, complete or partial interpretability will be asked by law and in addition to documentation, some audits might occur. Therefore, interpretability will become mandatory for many AI models.

### A.2. The developer or Data Scientist

The data scientist needs to access, as best as possible, the parameters of a model to understand it, to fix possible mistakes, to improve its performance and to stay in control.

#### A.3. The main user

The user needs information in order to best use the product or solution as well as to trust its recommendation. Adoption is driven by trust and very linked to the information that will be provided. We will get into more details about that in the Explicability section of these Guidelines.

#### A.4. The final user

The final user is the person who is the subject of the prediction or decision. That can be the same person as the main user or a different one. For example, we can have a skin diagnostic solution for doctors (main users) that concerns their patients (final users). This group of people may ask for information about the decision/prediction of the algorithm and in many case, they have the legal right to know what were the factor that lead to the decision/prediction.

# B. Of what?

We can be interested to have interpretability of the whole system or just of different parts of the system. We can look to understand a single prediction, all predictions, a part of the model, the whole model, the path that the data takes along the processing pipeline ...

## B.1. Global and Local

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.3:
There are two main information that we want to access when we talk about Interpretability. We want to access what factors influenced each prediction and with what extend, that is called the Local Interpretability, and we also want to know what the factors of importance for the model are, that is the Global Interpretability.

In the case of simple algorithms like the regressions, the global interpretability is directly given by the coefficients of the model after training (remember to scale or normalize your data first, overwise you will not be able to interpret the results). With sklearn, just use 'model.coef_'. You can put them in a DataFrame with the variable names for simpler visualization :

pd. DataFrame ( {"Feature" : X_train . columns . tolist () , "Coefficients" : model . coef [0]})

In the case of more complex models, we will need to use additional tools to have access to an approximation of this information, like Lime or Shap or the Feature Importance function of sklearn. N.B. LIME and SHAP are local interpretability methods but they can still be used for the global interpretability.

You can refer to the sklearn MOOC part on feature importance here for more

information: Feature importance - Scikit-learn course

For the interpretability of the predictions (local), we see the same distinction between simple and more complex algorithms. With the simple models, we can directly apply the coefficients or the rules (for simple decision tree) to the data specific for each individual prediction. For more complex algorithms, we will again use additional tools (Lime, Shap ... ).

We will come back to the tools in more details in a dedicated section.

# C. Why not interpretable?

## C.1. The data complexity

There are different ways in which the data itself can make the system hard to interpret.

Some variables may contain information that is difficult to comprehend, for example medical or scientific information or expert technical measurements.

Moreover, during the Feature Engineering of the model construction, we may apply manipulations to the data that may lead to a loss of meaning. For example taking the log of a measure, using a dimension reduction method (i.e. PCA), combining variables ...

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.4:
Finally, the storage of the data can add to the complexity if we lose the lineage and the contextual information that goes with it.

## C.2. The mathematical complexity

As quickly mentioned earlier, a few algorithms are directly interpretable by their mathematical nature. There are 3 of them: the linear regression, the logistic regression and the simple decision tree. By getting the regression coefficients we have direct access to the feature importance foe the model (global interpretability) and we can apply those coefficients to each prediction to get the local interpretability. The decision tree also gives us directly the underlying rules of the model.

On the other hand, many algorithms are not directly interpretable, that is the case of most models used : Ensemble models (Random Forest, XGBoost ... ), SVM, Artificial Neural Networks ... In these cases, we have to use additional toolboxes like Lime or Shap (that we will describe later) to access the model and prediction interpretability.

It is important to note that already existing regulations (for finance and insurance for example) and the one to come from the UE impose the use of directly interpretable models for critical systems i.e. the ones with a potential effect on users' interests.

## C.3. Direct interpretability

## C.3.a. Regressions

You get the direct interpretability for regressions (either for classifications or regressions) by looking at the coefficients. You get the coefficient in Sklearn with 'model.coef_'. You can put them in a DataFrame with the variable names for simpler visualization : pd. DataFrame ( {"Feature": X_train . columns . tolist () , "Coefficients" : model. coef [0]}

Don't forget to standardize your data first!

You can normalize your data by centering or scaling

First, normalizing allows interpretation of variable coefficients associated with different magnitudes, e.g. when one regressor is measured on a very small order, while another on a very large order. Standardization allows the units of regression coefficients to be expressed in the same units. Luckily, centering or scaling does not have an impact on p-values, therefore regression model statistics can be interpreted the same way as if centering or scaling did not take place.

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.5:
Second, when regression is used for explaining a phenomenon, interpreting the y- intercept is important. Centering results in predictors having a mean of zero means that the intercept can be assumed to have the expected value Y when the predictor values are set to the mean (zero). Without centering, the predictors would have to be set to zero to interpret the Y intercept, which may not be feasible or sensible.

Third, when creating sums or averages of variables on different scale, it may be important to scale the variables to have the same unit.

During standardization, we remove the mean from each value, and then scale them by dividing them by their standard deviation. The result is data with a mean of zero and the standard deviation of one.

Sklearn in Python offers several scalers: StandardScaler, MinMaxScaler, MaxAbsScaler and RobustScaler ( 6.3. Preprocessing data ).

This is an example of how to use the Standard Scaler: from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler scaler = StandardScaler () scaled_df=pd. DataFrame (scaler. fit_transform (df) )

## C.3.b. Decision Trees

Decision Trees is the second type of directly interpretable models. You can use it for either Classification or Regression problems.

Once trained, you can get the information about the importance of the features in different ways using sklearn.

## C.4. The ensemble complexity

It is important to note that combining directly interpretable models (weak learners) induces the loss of the interpretability. For example, tree-based ensemble models by bagging (random forest) or boosting (adaboost, gradient boosting, XGboost) are not directly interpretable even if based on simple decision trees, as each tree only sees a subpart of the data and that their outputs are combined.

Stacking models also makes the system non-directly interpretable, even if, once again, every stacked models are simple models.

## C.5. Performance/Interpretability Trade-off

Often, more complex models give better performance. Especially for complex datasets, XGBoost for example will perform better than a simple decision tree. But that added complexity leads to the loss of the interpretability. We talk about the Performance / Interpretability Trade-off.

Source: figure from Arrieta et al. 2019 that you can find here: Re (PDF) Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI): Concepts, Taxonomies, Opportunities and Challenges toward Responsible AI

We highly recommend reading this paper for more information on the subject.

There is no general guideline to follow to resolve this trade-off, it is a case-by-case consideration to make. Depending on the risk/criticality for the users of the model you are building and the regulation in place, you might not have a choice to go for a directly interpretable algorithm. In the other cases, it is always better to start simple and to increase the complexity until reaching a satisfying level of performance.

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.8:
# D. Toolboxes for indirect interpretability

There are many tools to 'open the black box' and get information about the model, but keep in mind that there are all indirect variable estimations and are just indications and certainly not exact information.

Most of these techniques are universal (model agnostic) and can be applied to all algorithms/models. However, certain tools are better suited for certain types of data and certain type of models. We will mainly focus on two toolboxes: Lime and SHAP as there are the ones most widely used.

## D.1. Structured data

For structured data, and for models that are not directly interpretable, we suggest the use of these two toolboxes, LIME and Shap.

## D.1.a. LIME

The acronym LIME stands for Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations. It provides local interpretability for your classifications and can be applied to any model. It is based on this paper : https://arxiv.org/pdf/1602.04938.pdf and you can also watch this video for a high-level explanation : KDD2016 paper 573

LIME uses a local surrogate model trained on perturbations of the data point we are investigating for explanations.

Source : https://github.com/marcotcr/lime

Even though we can use any interpretable model as the local surrogate, the paper uses a Lasso regression to induce sparsity in explanations.

The advantages of the technique are:

· Both the methodology and the explanations are very intuitive to explain to a human being.

· It is Model-agnostic.

. It works for structured as well as unstructured data (more on that later).

· Available in R and Python (original implementation by the authors of the paper).

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.9:
Here is how the algorithm works:

· In order to find an explanation for a single data point and a given classifier, it samples the locality around the selected single data point uniformly and at random and generate a dataset of perturbed data points with its corresponding prediction from the model we want to explain.

· Then it uses the specified feature selection methodology to select the number of features that is required for the explanation.

· It calculates the sample weights using a kernel function and a distance function (this captures how close or how far are the sampled points from the original point).

· It then fits an interpretable model (the Lasso regression) on the perturbed dataset using the sample weights to weigh the objective function.

· Finally, it provides local explanations using the newly trained interpretable model.

It provides nice visualizations of the local interpretability like this example taken from this tutorial : Tutorial - continuous and categorical features from LIME's authors:

This is analyzing the Iris dataset, with a Random Forest, after training an 'explainer' like that:

explainer = lime. lime_tabular. LimeTabularExplainer (train, feature_names=iris. feature_names,

class_names=iris. target_names, discretize_continuous=True)

exp = explainer. explain_instance (test [i], rf.predict_proba, num_features=2, top_labels=1)

This figure tells you that, for this instance,(1) the Random Forest model predicted the label 'setosa' with a probability of 1 (left part of the image), (2) it did no because the petal width was smaller or equal to 0.30cm and its length less than 1.6cm (central part of the image) and (3) it gives you the feature values highlighting the important features.

For more details, you can look at:

. The tutorial: Tutorial - continuous and categorical features

· This article: https://towardsdatascience.com/lime-how-to-interpret-machine- learning-models-with-python-94b0e7e4432e

<!-- PageFooter: D.1.b. SHAP -->
<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.10:
SHAP stands for SHapley Additive explanations. They define their approach as: 'is a game theoretic approach to explain the output of any machine learning model. It connects optimal credit allocation with local explanations using the classic Shapley values from game theory and their related extensions'. We are going to try to be a bit clearer but it is definitely less intuitive than LIME.

Game theory is a theoretical framework for social situations among competing players. It is the science of optimal decision-making of independent and competing actors in a strategic setting. A "Game" is any situation in which there are several decision-makers, and each of them wants to optimize their results. The optimizing decision will depend on the decisions of others. The game identifies the players' identities, preferences, and available strategies and how these strategies affect the outcome.

In essence, Game Theory is a way to mathematically model complex human behavior, to try to understand it, and predict it. You can read more about game theory and it's fit in the AI world in this article : Game (Theory) for AI? An Illustrated Guide for Everyone .

Applied to Machine Learning, we know that each value of an independent variable or a feature for a given sample is a part of a cooperative game where we assume that prediction is actually the payout. So, let's use an example to understand this in detail.

Assume the following scenario:

We have trained a machine learning model to predict apartment prices in Paris. For a certain apartment, our model predicts 250K euros and we need to explain this prediction. This apartment is 75m2, 2 bedrooms, on the 3rd floor. The average prediction for all apartments is 210K euros. How much has each feature value contributed to the prediction compared to the average prediction?

Now, if we talk in terms of Game Theory, the "game" here is the prediction task for a single instance of the dataset. The "players" are the feature values of the instance that collaborate to play the game (predict a value).

In our example, the feature values 'square_meters, nb_bedrooms and floor' worked together to achieve the prediction of 250K euros. Our goal is to explain the difference between the actual prediction (250K euros) and the average prediction (210K euros): a difference of 40K euros.

A possible explanation could be 'square_meters' contributed 20K euros, 'nb_bedrooms' contributed 10K euros, and 'floor' contributed 10K euros. The contributions add up to 40K - the prediction minus the average predicted house price.

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.11:
The Shapley value for each variable (payout) is basically trying to find the correct weight such that the sum of all Shapley values is the difference between the predictions and average value of the model. In other words, Shapley values correspond to the contribution of each feature towards pushing the prediction away from the expected value.

Here is the intuitive explanation of the Shapley values, for more details please refer to :

· The Shap librairy documentation:

Welcome to the SHAP documentation - SHAP latest documentation

· The github page:

GitHub - shap/shap: A game theoretic approach to explain the output of any machine learning model.

· A good article explaining how it works:

Interpretable Machine Learning using SHAP-theory and applications

· And this article for a slightly different view:

Understanding SHAP for Interpretable Machine Learning

Computing shapley values takes more time than LIME, but the library comes with more useful types of visualizations that we will describe here. The examples used are coming from their github (GitHub - shap/shap: A game theoretic approach to explain the output of any machine learning model. )

There are different methods of shapley values approximation depending on the type of method we are trying to explain : KernelSHAP, DeepSHAP and TreeSHAP.

First of all, using SHAP is extremely easy, and it can be used on any model. Here is a snipet of code for XGBoost on the Boston Housing Dataset: import xgboost

import shap # train an XGBoost model

X, y = shap.datasets.boston ()

model = xgboost. XGBRegressor () . fit (X, y)

# explain the model's predictions using SHAP (same syntax works for LightGBM, CatBoost, scikit-learn, transformers, Spark, etc.) explainer = shap. Explainer (model)

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.12:
shap_values = explainer (X)

The first interesting plot is the Waterfall: # visualize the first prediction's explanation shap.plots.waterfall (shap_values [0] )

You can see the influence of each feature, with their direction and magnitude.

Another, more condensed way to illustrate the same thing is the 'force plot': # visualize the first prediction's explanation with a force plot shap.plots. force (shap_values [0])

This is probably the most used visualization of the library.

To understand how a single feature effects the output of the model, we can plot the SHAP value of that feature vs. the value of the feature for all the examples in a dataset. It is a similar approach to Partial Dependency Plot (PDP). In the example here, we are looking at the effect of the number of rooms ('RM') # create a dependence scatter plot to show the effect of a single feature across

the whole dataset shap. plots . scatter (shap_values [ :, "RM"], color=shap_values)

Since SHAP values represent a feature's responsibility for a change in the model output, this plot represents the change in predicted house price as RM (the average number of rooms per house in an area) changes. You can try to understand the variability observed for a single value of RM with its interaction effects with other features. To help reveal these interactions we can color by another feature. If we pass the whole explanation tensor to the color argument the scatter plot will pick the best feature to color by. In this case it picks RAD (index of accessibility to radial highways) since that highlights that the average number of rooms per house has less impact on home price for areas with a high RAD value.

SHAP can also give you the global interpretability of your model.

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.13:
To do so, we can plot the SHAP values of every feature for every sample. This can be done with the Beeswarm plot. It sorts features by the sum of SHAP value magnitudes over all samples, and uses SHAP values to show the distribution of the impacts each feature has on the model output. The color represents the feature value (red high, blue low). This reveals for example that a high LSTAT (% lower status of the population) lowers the predicted home price. # summarize the effects of all the features shap.plots.beeswarm (shap_values)

Finally, we can also just take the mean absolute value of the SHAP values for each feature to get a standard 'feature importance' bar plot: shap.plots. bar (shap_values)

It is important to note that SHAP designed specific solution for Tree based models (all the way to XGBoost), with their shap.TreeExplainer(model).

We won't go into the details here, but you can find the documentation here : Tabular examples - SHAP latest documentation, and their Nature paper on the subject here: https://www.nature.com/articles/s42256-019-0138-9

Overall, SHAP is a very good library, offering many ways to visualize your interpretability compared to LIME, so why consider LIME? As we already mentioned, the advantage of LIME over SHAP is SPEED. The two methods emerge very differently. LIME perturbs data around an individual prediction to build a model, while SHAP has to compute all permutations globally to get local accuracy.

## D.1.c. Other methods

They are many other interpretability methods, we will not get into the details, we will refer you to this online book by Christoph Molnar: Interpretable Machine Learning

We will still list them here to be exhaustive.

### Global Model-Agnostic Methods:

· The partial dependence plot is a feature effect method.

· Accumulated local effect plots is another feature effect method that works when features are dependent.

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.14:
· Feature interaction (H-statistic) quantifies to what extent the prediction is the result of joint effects of the features.

· Functional decomposition is a central idea of interpretability and a technique that decomposes the complex prediction function into smaller parts.

· Permutation feature importance measures the importance of a feature as an increase in loss when the feature is permuted.

· Global surrogate models replaces the original model with a directly interpretable model for interpretation.

· Prototypes and criticisms are representative data point of a distribution and can be used to enhance interpretability.

Local Model-Agnostic Methods:

· Individual conditional expectation curves are the building blocks for partial dependence plots and describe how changing a feature changes the prediction.

· Scoped rules (anchors) are rules that describe which feature values anchor a prediction, in the sense that they lock the prediction in place.

· Counterfactual explanations explain a prediction by examining which features would need to be changed to achieve a desired prediction.

· Shapley values are an attribution method that fairly assigns the prediction to individual features.

### And of course:

· Local surrogate models (LIME) explain a prediction by replacing the complex model with a locally interpretable surrogate model.

· SHAP is another computation method for Shapley values, but also proposes global interpretation methods based on combinations of Shapley values across the data.

If you don't want to read a whole book, here are two papers reviewing the subject:

· PNAS

· NCBI - WWW Error Blocked Diagnostic

#### D.2. Text

We have seen the different toolboxes and methods for tabular, structured data, but how can you do for text? It can be very important to know what words have been critical in order to: assign a sentiment to the sentence, categorize a text or whatever your NLP task was.

LIME and SHAP propose solutions specific to NLP. We will look into them and add another option too.

## D.2.a. LIME

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.15:
lime

The same way that LIME has a tabular explainer, it also has a text explainer ( package - lime 0.1 documentation ).

You initiate the explainer, after having trained a model for sentient analysis for example:

class_names=['negative', 'positive'] explainer = LimeTextExplainer(class_names=class_names)

And you get the explainer to explain an instance ('text'):

exp = explainer. explain_instance(text, model. predict_proba, num_features=6) And you get that illustration:

It gives you the usual LIME plot with the prediction probabilities of the model for this prediction (on the left) and what were the features (here words) important for the prediction.

This important addition is the highlighted text at the bottom of the figure, that shows you the words, in the sentence, that were important, color coded as a function of the possible labels (here blue for positive, orange for negative sentiment).

As for tabular data, LIME is easy to use and relatively fast.

## D.2.b. SHAP

The process with SHAP is very similar, there are just more visualization options. You

can access the documentation here : Text examples - SHAP latest documentation , from which we took the following examples.

You start with the explainer on your pretrained model: explainer = shap. Explainer (pred)

You compute the shap values for a specific instance: shap_values = explainer (data [ ' text ' ] [ : 0] )

And you visualize the result: shap. plots. text (shap_values)

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.16:
You have here the different possible labels at the top, with the predicted label highlighted, then the classical SHAP force plot in the middle, and the sentence with important words highlighted at the bottom.

Additionally, you can look at the important words for a single class ('joy' in this example) by averaging the shap values.

shap.plots.bar(shap_values[ :,:,"joy"].mean(0))

For further understanding of the use of SHAP for NLP, you can read this paper: https://openreview.net/pdf?id=yqu82n5FZ9r

### D.2.c. Attention Interpretability

Quite often nowadays, deep neural networks are used for NLP tasks. This allows for specific interpretability if you use attention networks. Indeed, attention layer in a neural network can give you insight into the model's prediction by obtaining the weights in a specific layer. It works for example with BERT and other Transformers.

The interpretability power of these methods is under discussion, we won't go into the details here, but you should know it is a possibility.

Here are some more or less scientific papers about this:

· https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.11218.pdf

. https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2021/papers/Chefer_Transformer_ Interpretability_Beyond_Attention_Visualization_CVPR_2021_paper.pdf

· Deconstructing BERT, Part 2: Visualizing the Inner Workings of Attention

· https://arxiv.org/pdf/2108.04840.pdf

## D.3. Images

As for text, images also have specific functions from LIME and SHAP, and also, attention layers of some Deep Learning Networks can be used.

## D.3.a. LIME

As for tabular data, LIME generates perturbations in order to see the impact on the model decision and see what part of the image is crucial for the prediction. Here is an illustration of the process:

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.17:
The way to implement LIME for image is similar to everything we have seen before. Here we use an example for their official tutorial (Lime Images ), which is based on inception v3. We will look at the interpretability of this image:

The multilabel predictions for the top 5 classes are: 286 Egyptian cat 0.000892741

242 EntleBucher 0.0163564

239 Greater Swiss Mountain dog 0.0171362 241 Appenzeller 0.0393639 240 Bernese mountain dog 0.829222

Let's look at how to visualize the interpretation. First initiate the Explainer and get the explanation:

explainer = lime_image. LimeImageExplainer () explanation = explainer . explain_instance (image, predict_fn, top_labels=5, hide_color=0, num_samples=1000)

Now let see the explanation for the top class (Bernese mountain dog)

from skimage. segmentation import mark_boundaries

temp, mask = explanation. get_image_and_mask(240, positive_only=True, num_features=5, hide_rest=True) plt.imshow(mark_boundaries(temp / 2 + 0.5, mask))

We can also look at the part of the image in favor (green) and in disfavor (red) of the top class:

temp, mask = explanation.get_image_and_mask (240, positive_only=False, num_features=10, hide_rest=False) plt.imshow (mark_boundaries (temp / 2 + 0.5, mask) )

You will find more information on this and the rest of LIME in their paper: https://arxiv.org/pdf/1602.04938.pdf

SHAP

There are two ways to get interpretability of images with SHAP.

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.18:
The first one, is with a DeepExplainer. Here is an example using the MNIST databased and a CNN model.

import shap import numpy as np # select a set of background examples to take an expectation over background = x_train [np. random. choice (x_train. shape [0], 100, replace=False) ]

# explain predictions of the model on four images e = shap. DeepExplainer (model, background) # ... or pass tensors directly # e = shap. DeepExplainer ( (model . layers [0] . input, model . layers [-1] . output) , background) shap_values = e. shap_values (x_test [1: 5] ) # plot the feature attributions shap. image_plot (shap_values, -x_test [1:5] )

The image_plot function displays the explanations of the 10 possible outputs (digits 0- 9) for four different instances. In red you have the pixels that increased the model's prediction for a specific output while blue pixels are going against that output. Remember that the sum of the SHAP values equals the difference between the expected model output (averaged over the whole dataset) and the current model output. Interestingly, we can then understand why, for the 'zero' image the blank middle is important, while for the 'four' image the lack of a connection on top makes it a four instead of a nine (see the blue pixels for the '4' image for the '9' explainer).

The second possible way to use SHAP for images is to use the GradientExplainer. It works with TensorFlow, Keras and PyTorch models.

Here the example explains how the 7th intermediate layer of the VGG16 ImageNet model impacts the output probabilities. from keras.applications.vgg16 import VGG16

from keras. applications.vgg16 import preprocess_input import keras.backend as K import numpy as np import json import shap # load pre-trained model and choose two images to explain model = VGG16 (weights='imagenet', include_top=True)

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.19:
X, y = shap.datasets. imagenet50 () to_explain = X [ [39,41] ]

# load the ImageNet class names url = "https://s3.amazonaws. com/deep-learning-models/image- models/imagenet_class_index. json" fname = shap.datasets. cache (url) with open (fname) as f:

class_names = json. load (f)

# explain how the input to the 7th layer of the model explains the top two classes def map2layer (x, layer) : feed_dict = dict (zip ( [model. layers [0] .input], [preprocess_input (x. copy () ) ] ) ) return K.get_session () . run (model . layers [ layer] . input, feed_dict) e = shap. GradientExplainer ( (model. layers [7] . input, model. layers [-1] .output) , map2layer (X, 7), local_smoothing=0 # std dev of smoothing noise ) shap_values, indexes = e. shap_values (map2layer (to_explain, 7) , ranked_outputs=2) # get the names for the classes index_names = np. vectorize (lambda x: class_names [str (x) ] [1] ) (indexes) # plot the explanations shap. image_plot (shap_values, to_explain, index_names)

These examples were taken from SHAP's github that you can find here: GitHub - shap/shap: A game theoretic approach to explain the output of any machine learning model.

## D.3.c. Attention

For images, LIME and SHAP are not the most common techniques for interpretability. There are specific ways to look into the role played by different pixels in a CNN model for images classification. We will describe some of the here.

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.20:
### D.3.c.i. Saliency maps

Saliency map is an explanation method used for interpreting the predictions of convolutional neural networks (CNNs). This is probably the oldest and the most frequently used method of interpretation in deep learning. Basically, the saliency map of an input image specifies parts of it that contribute the most to the activity of a specific layer in the network, or the decision of the network as a whole.

There are three main approaches to getting the saliency map of an input image for a CNN. The first proposed approach is using deconvolutional networks introduced by Zeiler and Fergus in 2013 (Visualizing and Understanding Convolutional Networks ). In this paper, to recognize which features (which are basically the pixels) in the input image an intermediate layer of the network is looking for, the authors have proposed a deconvolutional network that reconstructs the input from the activation of that layer. We won't go into the details, you can read the paper, but the whole process can be depicted as in the following figure:

Figure from Zeiler and Fergus in 2013 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1311.2901)

Here are some of the results of the reconstruction of the input image from the activations of the fifth layer of ZFNet:

Figure from Zeiler and Fergus in 2013 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1311.2901)

The second and probably the most straightforward approach of getting a saliency map is using the backpropagation algorithm to compute the gradients of logits relative to the input of the network (note that in the backpropagation algorithm used for training the network, the gradients are computed with regards to the parameters of the network). This approach was first proposed by Simonyan et al. 2013 (Deep Inside Convolutional Networks: Visualising Image ... ). Using backpropagation, we can highlight pixels of the input image based on the amount of the gradient they receive, which shows their contribution to the final score:

Figure from Simonyan et al. 2013 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1312.6034)

Springenberg et al. 2014 (Striving for Simplicity: The All Convolutional Net ) combined these first two approaches and proposed the guided backpropagation algorithm as the third way of getting saliency maps. In fact, instead of masking the importance signal based on the positions of negative values of the input signal in forward-pass

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.21:
(backpropagation) or the negative values of the reconstruction signal flowing from top to bottom (deconvolution), they mask the signal if each one of these cases occurs. The following figure depicts the guided backpropagation algorithm very well:

Figure from Springenberg et al. 2014 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.6806)

Here are some of the saliency maps produced by the guided backpropagation method. As can be seen - and reported in other works too -, this algorithm is quite good at capturing high-resolution and sharp saliency maps:

Figure from Springenberg et al. 2014 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.6806)

We can easily see that the saliency map is a local gradient-based backpropagation interpretation method. Although saliency maps are mostly used for interpreting CNNs, as the concept of gradient exists in all neural networks, one can use it for any arbitrary artificial neural network. Thus, it could be considered as a model-agnostic interpretation method. A similar approach is used by SHAP gradient explainer that we saw earlier.

Finally, it is worth noting that there a couple of papers that have shown saliency maps are not always reliable. Kindermans et al. 2018 (The (Un)reliability of saliency methods ) demonstrate that data preprocessing, such as subtracting the mean and normalization, can make undesirable changes in saliency maps. Ghorbani et al. 2019 (Interpretation of Neural Networks is Fragile ) also show that saliency maps are vulnerable to adversarial attacks.

D.3.c.ii. Class Activation Mapping (CAM) and GRADient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM)

Class activation map (CAM) is another explanation method used for CNNs, introduced by Zhou et al. 2016 (Learning Deep Features for Discriminative Localization ). The authors of the paper have evaluated networks with the architecture similar to the Network in Network's architecture (Network In Network ). In these networks, the stack of fully connected layers at the very end of the model has been replaced by a layer named Global Average Pooling (GAP). GAP simply averages the activations of each feature map and concatenates these averages and outputs them as a vector. Then, a weighted sum of this vector is fed to the final softmax loss layer. Using this architecture, we can highlight the important regions of the image by projecting back the weights of the output on the convolutional feature maps. The following figure illustrates this process:

<!-- PageBreak -->
pages.22:
<!-- PageHeader: Figure from Zhou et al. 2016 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1512.04150) -->

An example of Class Activation Map. The figure is borrowed from Zhu et al. 2017 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1709.01829).

Grad-CAM is a more versatile version of CAM that can produce visual explanations for any arbitrary CNN, even if the network contains a stack of fully connected layers too (e.g. the VGG networks).

In order to get the Grad-CAM of a given image and a class of interest, we can take a quite similar approach to what we took in computing saliency maps; we let the gradients of any target concept score (logits for any class of interest such as 'human's face') flow into the final convolutional layer. We can then compute an importance score based on the gradients and produce a coarse localization map highlighting the important regions in the image for predicting that concept.

The following figure shows a visualization produced by GRAD-CAM:

GRAD-CAM visualization of an example image for the class of 'Dog'. The figure is borrowed from Selvaraju et al. 2016 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1610.02391)

As Grad-CAM can only produce coarse-grained visualizations, the authors have also combined guided-backpropagation with their method and propose Guided Grad- CAM. They simply do that by an element-wise multiplication of guided- backpropagation visualization and Grad-CAM's visualization:

Guided Grad-CAM as a combination of Grad-CAM and Guided Backprop. The figure is borrowed from Selvaraju et al. 2016 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/1610.02391.pdf) with some modifications.

For more on CAM and Grad-CAM, you can read : Interpretability in Deep Learning M

M

With Weights & Biases: CAM and Grad-CAM or D L Grad-CAM: Visual Explanations from Deep Networks

full_text:
# 3- Interpretability

Machine Learning changed the way computer programs are done. Developers used to formalize a problem and code the rules that would transform input data into output data. Now, it is the model that determines the rules that link the input and output data through iterations and error minimization. It is this ability that gives all its power to Machine Learning and allows us to solve problems that exceed human formalization capabilities. But it is also coupled with a constraint: the rules applied by the model after training to make its predictions and decision may evade human control. For certain types of models, these rules sometimes remain unknown, even if interpretability techniques have made great progress in recent years. This is the famous "black box effect". However, when we decide to use Al to make decisions or make predictions that will have an impact on the lives of citizens, it is crucial, and soon to be regulatory, to keep control of the models and guarantee their explicability.

The interpretability of AI models is therefore becoming a major challenge, both for those who design them and for those who use them. It is a question of knowing which factors influence each decision and prediction (local interpretability) but also which rules govern the model in general (global interpretability).

Interpretability can be defined as having access to the features or variables that are driving the decisions or predictions of a model.

An Interpretable system becomes:

· Explicable: we can provide information and explanation (see the Explicability section)

· Transparent: it has a functioning intelligible to a human

· Provable: it can provide proof of the accuracy of the result

Whatever your use-case, it is critical to use interpretability methods.

If your system is critical, you should use directly interpretable models (regressions or simple decision trees), you can refer to Section 3.C.Direct Interpretability for more information.

If you are not required Direct Interpretability, are decided to use more complex models, you can use toolboxes to access indirect interpretability. You can refer to Section 3.D for more information, depending on the type of data (tabular, text or images).

## A. For whom?

<!-- PageBreak -->

### A.1. The regulator

As mentioned in the section about legal risks in Introduction, the new European law on AI will describe different levels of criticality for AI models. Depending on the level, complete or partial interpretability will be asked by law and in addition to documentation, some audits might occur. Therefore, interpretability will become mandatory for many AI models.

### A.2. The developer or Data Scientist

The data scientist needs to access, as best as possible, the parameters of a model to understand it, to fix possible mistakes, to improve its performance and to stay in control.

#### A.3. The main user

The user needs information in order to best use the product or solution as well as to trust its recommendation. Adoption is driven by trust and very linked to the information that will be provided. We will get into more details about that in the Explicability section of these Guidelines.

#### A.4. The final user

The final user is the person who is the subject of the prediction or decision. That can be the same person as the main user or a different one. For example, we can have a skin diagnostic solution for doctors (main users) that concerns their patients (final users). This group of people may ask for information about the decision/prediction of the algorithm and in many case, they have the legal right to know what were the factor that lead to the decision/prediction.

# B. Of what?

We can be interested to have interpretability of the whole system or just of different parts of the system. We can look to understand a single prediction, all predictions, a part of the model, the whole model, the path that the data takes along the processing pipeline ...

## B.1. Global and Local

<!-- PageBreak -->

There are two main information that we want to access when we talk about Interpretability. We want to access what factors influenced each prediction and with what extend, that is called the Local Interpretability, and we also want to know what the factors of importance for the model are, that is the Global Interpretability.

In the case of simple algorithms like the regressions, the global interpretability is directly given by the coefficients of the model after training (remember to scale or normalize your data first, overwise you will not be able to interpret the results). With sklearn, just use 'model.coef_'. You can put them in a DataFrame with the variable names for simpler visualization :

pd. DataFrame ( {"Feature" : X_train . columns . tolist () , "Coefficients" : model . coef [0]})

In the case of more complex models, we will need to use additional tools to have access to an approximation of this information, like Lime or Shap or the Feature Importance function of sklearn. N.B. LIME and SHAP are local interpretability methods but they can still be used for the global interpretability.

You can refer to the sklearn MOOC part on feature importance here for more

information: Feature importance - Scikit-learn course

For the interpretability of the predictions (local), we see the same distinction between simple and more complex algorithms. With the simple models, we can directly apply the coefficients or the rules (for simple decision tree) to the data specific for each individual prediction. For more complex algorithms, we will again use additional tools (Lime, Shap ... ).

We will come back to the tools in more details in a dedicated section.

# C. Why not interpretable?

## C.1. The data complexity

There are different ways in which the data itself can make the system hard to interpret.

Some variables may contain information that is difficult to comprehend, for example medical or scientific information or expert technical measurements.

Moreover, during the Feature Engineering of the model construction, we may apply manipulations to the data that may lead to a loss of meaning. For example taking the log of a measure, using a dimension reduction method (i.e. PCA), combining variables ...

<!-- PageBreak -->

Finally, the storage of the data can add to the complexity if we lose the lineage and the contextual information that goes with it.

## C.2. The mathematical complexity

As quickly mentioned earlier, a few algorithms are directly interpretable by their mathematical nature. There are 3 of them: the linear regression, the logistic regression and the simple decision tree. By getting the regression coefficients we have direct access to the feature importance foe the model (global interpretability) and we can apply those coefficients to each prediction to get the local interpretability. The decision tree also gives us directly the underlying rules of the model.

On the other hand, many algorithms are not directly interpretable, that is the case of most models used : Ensemble models (Random Forest, XGBoost ... ), SVM, Artificial Neural Networks ... In these cases, we have to use additional toolboxes like Lime or Shap (that we will describe later) to access the model and prediction interpretability.

It is important to note that already existing regulations (for finance and insurance for example) and the one to come from the UE impose the use of directly interpretable models for critical systems i.e. the ones with a potential effect on users' interests.

## C.3. Direct interpretability

## C.3.a. Regressions

You get the direct interpretability for regressions (either for classifications or regressions) by looking at the coefficients. You get the coefficient in Sklearn with 'model.coef_'. You can put them in a DataFrame with the variable names for simpler visualization : pd. DataFrame ( {"Feature": X_train . columns . tolist () , "Coefficients" : model. coef [0]}

Don't forget to standardize your data first!

You can normalize your data by centering or scaling

First, normalizing allows interpretation of variable coefficients associated with different magnitudes, e.g. when one regressor is measured on a very small order, while another on a very large order. Standardization allows the units of regression coefficients to be expressed in the same units. Luckily, centering or scaling does not have an impact on p-values, therefore regression model statistics can be interpreted the same way as if centering or scaling did not take place.

<!-- PageBreak -->

Second, when regression is used for explaining a phenomenon, interpreting the y- intercept is important. Centering results in predictors having a mean of zero means that the intercept can be assumed to have the expected value Y when the predictor values are set to the mean (zero). Without centering, the predictors would have to be set to zero to interpret the Y intercept, which may not be feasible or sensible.

Third, when creating sums or averages of variables on different scale, it may be important to scale the variables to have the same unit.

During standardization, we remove the mean from each value, and then scale them by dividing them by their standard deviation. The result is data with a mean of zero and the standard deviation of one.

Sklearn in Python offers several scalers: StandardScaler, MinMaxScaler, MaxAbsScaler and RobustScaler ( 6.3. Preprocessing data ).

This is an example of how to use the Standard Scaler: from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler scaler = StandardScaler () scaled_df=pd. DataFrame (scaler. fit_transform (df) )

## C.3.b. Decision Trees

Decision Trees is the second type of directly interpretable models. You can use it for either Classification or Regression problems.

Once trained, you can get the information about the importance of the features in different ways using sklearn.

You can print them as text : # Fit the classifier with default hyper-parameters clf = DecisionTreeClassifier (random_state=1234) model = clf. fit (X, y) text_representation = tree. export_text (clf) print (text_representation) feature 2 <= 2.45

Or plot the tree with The plot_tree method was added to sklearn in version 0.21. It requires matplotlib to be installed. It allows you to easily produce figure of the tree

(without intermediate exporting to graphviz). More information about it here : sklearn.tree.plot_tree

fig = plt.figure (figsize=(25,20)) = tree. plot_tree (clf, feature_names=iris. feature_names, class_names=iris. target_names, filled=True)
